ch01.title: The Evolution Path
ch01.intro: In 26 years, emoji grew from one mobile carrier's 176 12×12-pixel icons into a 3,790+ cross-platform, cross-cultural visual vocabulary.

chart.title: Unicode emoji total · 1999 → {lastYear}
chart.hint: Switch the window to zoom the dense modern range. Larger medallions mark decade milestones; dashed medallions are draft versions.
chart.ariaLabel: Line chart of cumulative Unicode emoji count over time. {count} version nodes visible in the current window.
chart.markerAria: {year} {version}: +{added} new emoji, {total} cumulative. Press Enter for details.

docomo-1999.narrative: Shigetaka Kurita designed 176 12×12-pixel icons for NTT DoCoMo's i-mode service.

iphone-jp-2007.narrative: SoftBank brought an emoji keyboard to Japanese iPhone users, accelerating emoji's spread across global mobile platforms.

emoji-6-0.narrative: 722 emojis enter the Unicode Standard, unifying the emoji sets from Japan's three major carriers.

emoji-7-0.narrative: Unicode 7.0 adds roughly 250 symbols — including Webdings and Wingdings characters — dramatically expanding the emoji vocabulary.

emoji-8-0.narrative: 5 Fitzpatrick skin-tone modifiers introduced; diverse family combinations gain visual representation.

emoji-11-0.narrative: 157 new emojis arrive including superheroes and hot/cold faces; the version number jumps from 5.0 to 11.0 to align with the Unicode Standard.

emoji-12-0.narrative: 230 new emojis (including variants) added, bringing gender-neutral figures and accessibility-themed characters into the standard.

emoji-12-1.narrative: 168 emojis updated, further expanding gender-neutral person sequences for greater inclusivity in human representation.

emoji-13-0.narrative: 117 new emojis debut, with 🥲 the smiling-face-with-tear capturing the bittersweet emotional register of the pandemic year.

emoji-13-1.narrative: 217 new sequences released, including heart-hand modifiers and cross-skin-tone handshakes, enriching human-connection expressions.

emoji-14-0.narrative: 112 new emojis bring heart-hands 🫶, melting face 🫠, and saluting face 🫡, deepening the gesture and emotion vocabulary.

emoji-15-0.narrative: 31 new emojis including pink heart 🩷 and shaking face 🫨 reflect the ongoing refinement of nuanced emotional expression.

emoji-15-1.narrative: 118 new emojis including direction-flipped face sequences demonstrate Unicode's support for multidirectional human-body motion.

emoji-16-0.narrative: Only 8 new emojis added, signaling a shift from expansion to refinement in the standardization cycle.

apple-genmoji.narrative: Apple's iOS 18 introduces Genmoji: natural-language-prompted personalized emoji.

emoji-17-0.narrative: 163 new emojis ship with Unicode 17.0 in September 2025. Headline new characters include the distorted face, fight cloud, orca, hairy creature, trombone, landslide, and treasure chest. The medallion 🫨 is a visual stand-in — 17.0 codepoints render as placeholder boxes until your system font is updated (rolling out through 2026).

emoji-18-0.narrative: Draft: 9 core new emojis + 10 skin-tone variants (19 total). Candidates include a cracking face, pickle, lighthouse, meteor, and new thumb gestures. Expected September 2026 release with Unicode 18.0. Medallion is a visual stand-in — draft codepoints and final glyphs are still subject to change.

ch02.title: Who Gets In
ch02.intro: About 100 proposals are submitted to Unicode each year. The acceptance rate is roughly 30–60% — and it hinges on more than technical merit.

freq.title: Frequency
freq.desc: Measurable usage via Google Trends, social-media search signals, and web-search volume — evidence that people actively want to send this emoji.

multi-use.title: Multiple Use Cases
multi-use.desc: Does the emoji carry metaphorical or symbolic meaning beyond its literal form? Versatile symbols (e.g., a rocket for ambition, a fire for excitement) score higher.

distinct.title: Distinctiveness
distinct.desc: Must be visually and semantically distinct from existing emoji, and recognisable at typical 18×18-pixel display sizes. No near-duplicates allowed.

representativeness.title: Cultural Representativeness
representativeness.desc: Fills a genuine gap for under-represented groups, cultures, foods, or traditions. The Unicode Consortium weighs whether a community's visual vocabulary is missing from the standard.

no-brand-deity.title: No Brands, No Deities
no-brand-deity.desc: Company logos, products associated with a specific brand, religious deities, and named individuals (living, historic, or fictional) are automatically declined.

short-term-fad.title: Not a Short-Term Fad
short-term-fad.desc: Transient or faddish concepts are poor candidates. The expected usage must be durable — not just a moment of viral popularity that will fade within a year.

dumpling.story: Designer Yiying Lu filed proposal L2/16-024 in January 2016 to add the dumpling — a staple across Chinese, Japanese, Korean, and Eastern-European cuisines — to Unicode. The proposal demonstrated multi-region usage frequency and broad cultural coverage. The emoji shipped with Unicode 10.0 in June 2017.

headscarf.proposer: Woman With Headscarf 🧕 · proposed by Rayouf Alhumedhi (L2/16-284)
headscarf.story: Then 15-year-old Rayouf Alhumedhi submitted proposal L2/16-284 in October 2016, arguing that hundreds of millions of Muslim women who wear a hijab had no emoji representation. The ESC cited the proposal's frequency data and clear representativeness gap. The emoji was approved and released with Unicode 10.0 in June 2017.

mate.proposer: Mate 🧉 · cultural drink from South America (L2/18-122)
mate.story: Proposal L2/18-122 made the case for yerba mate, the ubiquitous hot infused drink shared across Argentina, Uruguay, Brazil, and Paraguay. The proposal showed strong regional search volume and a cultural significance that no existing emoji captured. Mate entered the standard with Unicode 12.0 in March 2019.

deity-rejected.story: The Unicode Consortium's guidelines explicitly list 'Deities' as an automatically declined category. Proposals featuring Hindu, Buddhist, or other religious deities have been submitted but declined without evaluation, because encoding a deity could imply endorsement of one religion over others and raise irreversible controversy. The exclusion policy is published at unicode.org/emoji/proposals.html.

map.title: Where recent emoji came from
map.hint: Hover or focus a pin to see context; click to pin
map.ariaLabel: World map showing geographic origins of {count} culturally-representative emojis.
map.pinAria: {emoji} from {country}, added in {year}. Press Enter for details.

ch03.title: Always On
ch03.intro: Gen Z is the first generation that has never put the phone out of sight. For them, emoji aren't decoration — they're a new grammar, punctuation, and tone of voice.

semantic.heading: Same emoji, different meaning by generation
semantic.disclosure: Based on cited reporting and journalism — not a primary survey dataset.

dayInLife.heading: 24-hour phone-engagement intensity · generational comparison
dayInLife.caption: 24 hours on the x-axis; the y-axis shows each group's relative phone-engagement frequency (peak = 100%). The two curves track teens (Gen Z) versus all adults; you can see their peaks fall at different hours — teens have a steeper late-night ridge and a much later morning ramp-up, while adults follow a commute + lunch + early-evening rhythm.
dayInLife.methodology: Methodology: the curves are 24-point series reconstructed from descriptive findings in published reports (not raw published data points). The teen curve draws on Common Sense Media's 2021 Census (ages 8–18) findings about out-of-school usage intensity; the adult curve draws on Reviews.org's 2023 U.S. cellphone-usage survey findings about morning, lunch, and evening peaks. A fully reproducible hour-by-generation public dataset doesn't currently exist.
dayInLife.ariaLabel: 24-hour phone engagement comparison line chart with two curves: Gen Z and all adults.
dayInLife.cellAria: {hour}:00 hour — Gen Z around {genZ}%, all adults around {adults}%. Press Enter for details.

genZ.label: Gen Z / teens
genZ.desc: Ages 8–18. Peak engagement falls in out-of-school hours: after-school, evening, late-night.

allAdults.label: All adults
allAdults.desc: U.S. adult mixed sample. Peaks track the morning commute, lunch break, and early-evening leisure window.

ch04.title: Beyond the Screen
ch04.intro: 26 years on, emoji are escaping the keyboard — generated, dimensionalized, worn on faces, made into brands, and stepped into space.

apple-genmoji.title: Genmoji
apple-genmoji.story: iOS 18 generates personalized emoji from a natural-language prompt. Every user can now produce their own.

vision-pro-persona.title: Vision Pro Persona
vision-pro-persona.story: Apple Vision Pro maps a user's face and hand movements in real time onto a photorealistic 3D avatar — a Persona — bringing emoji-style self-expression from a flat screen into immersive spatial computing.

snap-bitmoji-ar.title: Bitmoji AR Lenses
snap-bitmoji-ar.story: Snap deeply integrated Bitmoji personal avatars with AR Lenses, letting users project their custom cartoon selves into the real world; by 2023, Bitmoji surpassed 250 million monthly active users.

world-emoji-day-brands.title: World Emoji Day Brand Activations
world-emoji-day-brands.story: Every July 17th, hundreds of brands — from Coca-Cola to McDonald's to Google — launch global social-media campaigns built around custom emoji; World Emoji Day has become the calendar's signature moment for corporate emoji marketing.

slack-emoji-workflow.title: Slack Emoji Workflow Triggers
slack-emoji-workflow.story: Slack's Workflow Builder lets teams use emoji reactions as automation triggers, turning a single-tap sticker into the starting point for cross-app business processes — making emoji a structured interface meta-language for workplace collaboration.

moma-docomo.title: MoMA Acquires the Original Emoji
moma-docomo.story: Shigetaka Kurita's 176 original 1999 emoji, designed for NTT DoCoMo's i-mode, were permanently acquired by the Museum of Modern Art in 2016 — placing a set of 12×12-pixel communication glyphs squarely inside the art-historical canon.

google-noto-emoji.title: Google Noto Emoji Open Font
google-noto-emoji.story: Launched with the goal of eliminating "tofu" (missing-character boxes), Google's Noto Emoji project provides an open-source, Unicode-compliant color emoji font maintained through Unicode 17.0 — serving as shared visual infrastructure for cross-platform emoji consistency.

github-emoji-reactions.title: GitHub Emoji Reactions API
github-emoji-reactions.story: GitHub added emoji reactions (👍 ❤️ 🚀 and more) to its REST API in 2016, enabling developers to annotate and filter issues, pull requests, and commits with structured emoji signals — turning a playful sticker into a machine-readable workflow primitive.

wechat-sticker-economy.title: WeChat Sticker Open Platform
wechat-sticker-economy.story: Tencent's WeChat sticker platform lets artists publish paid and free packs; cumulative downloads exceeded billions by 2024 — a uniquely Chinese-internet pattern of emoji personalization that spawned a thriving creator economy.

footer.heading: Sources & Methodology
footer.credits: Every quantitative claim on this page carries a public, citable source.
